ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific Earl Grey milk tea or less-sugar variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages with milk as the closest defensible category. No Earl Grey-specific or less-sugar variant code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Earl Grey milk tea. SNOMED CT does not enumerate beverage subtypes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of Earl Grey milk tea (less sugar) is approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing about 128 kcal, 22 g carbohydrate, and 14.8 g sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Earl Grey Milk Tea (Less Sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Earl Grey milk tea (less sugar), CR ID D08010020. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated using USDA FoodData Central analogous milk tea entry (NDB 14355) as a secondary reference for fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Earl Grey milk tea (less sugar). GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous entry: sweetened milk tea / tea with milk and sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). The standard GI classification scale defines low GI as ≤55, medium as 56–69, and high as ≥70. A GI of 55 therefore falls at the upper boundary of the low category. The 'less sugar' formulation reduces the sugar load relative to standard milk tea, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 14.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.1 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (5.92 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; sugar-sweetened beverages independently elevate blood pressure risk (AHA 2018)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its residual added sugar (5.92 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk even at low sodium levels; choose plain unsweetened Earl Grey tea instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 5.92 g/100 g with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugars are rapidly absorbed and cause acute postprandial glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Earl Grey milk tea (less sugar) as the 5.92 g/100 g sugar content with near-zero fibre produces rapid glycaemic excursions; plain unsweetened Earl Grey tea (0 g added sugar) is the recommended alternative per ADA beverage guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from creamer (~1.1 g/100 g) and added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) contribute to atherogenic risk; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased CVD mortality
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit consumption of this sweetened milk tea due to its combined saturated fat and added sugar content; substituting with unsweetened Earl Grey tea preserves the cardioprotective bergamot polyphenol benefit without the atherogenic sugar and fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Residual added sugar (5.92 g/100 g) with negligible fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory micronutrients promotes low-grade systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to unsweetened Earl Grey tea to obtain bergamot polyphenol anti-inflammatory benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load; verify creamer type as palm-based creamers add pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey Milk Tea (Less Sugar) is a popular Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by brewing black tea flavoured with bergamot oil and blending it with milk or non-dairy creamer, with a reduced amount of added sugar compared to the standard preparation. The drink is widely available at bubble tea shops, kopitiams, and café chains across Singapore and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or afternoon beverage. The flavour profile is floral and citrusy from the bergamot, with a creamy, mildly sweet finish. The 'less sugar' designation typically corresponds to approximately 70% of the standard sugar level used by the outlet. Per 100 g, this beverage provides 51 kcal, 0.24 g protein, 1.77 g fat, 8.82 g carbohydrate, and 5.92 g sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g) and low sodium (9 mg). Despite the reduced sugar label, the sugar content of 5.92 g/100 g still classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage under ADA and AHA guidelines, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The bergamot polyphenols in Earl Grey tea have demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties in peer-reviewed studies, but these benefits are substantially offset by the added sugar and creamer content in the milk tea preparation. Clinically, this beverage should be treated as a discretionary item for metabolic health conditions. Plain unsweetened Earl Grey tea is the recommended alternative for individuals seeking the polyphenol benefits without the glycaemic and cardiovascular risk. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low GI boundary) with a glycemic load of 8.1 per 250 ml serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D08010020) with confidence 0.95 as reported by the canonical source. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous milk tea entry as a secondary reference, documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.10 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.40 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.10 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous milk tea entry (NDB 14355 — Tea, ready-to-drink, with milk) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. Trans_fat_g set to 0.0 g as confirmed negligible in standard milk tea preparations. 2. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific less-sugar Earl Grey milk tea preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr); no direct GI measurement exists for Earl Grey milk tea (less sugar). Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 is classified as low GI. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to align with this boundary definition. 4. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input are preserved as contextually accurate (sodium 9 mg/100 g; energy 51 kcal/100 g) but do not override the clinical concern ratings for sugar content. 5. Halal status is assumed based on standard ingredients; individual outlet preparation may vary.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Populated all mandatory fields from HPB source data. Added Singaporean multilingual names. Added health context for all three required conditions.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match_type fields from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Confirmed gi_category as 'medium' for GI 55.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. Updated gi_source notes and main notes to document this correction. This resolves the q4 failure identified by the validator in iteration 2.
